matches.x: Whoever sheds the blood of man, by man shall his blood be shed; for in the image of God has God made man.” (Gen. 8:29–9:6)

Said R. Hiya: ‘It is written: “Whoso sheddeth man’s blood, by man shall his blood be shed; for in the image of God made he man” (Gen. 9, 6). He who sheds the blood of a fellow-man is thus considered as diminishing the Divine archetype of man as well. Thus the first commandment, “I am the Lord thy God”, contains the motive for the sixth, “Thou shalt not murder.”

matches.x: Why is it that the sacred writer has not simply said, Ham saw his nakedness, but Ham the father of Canaan saw the nakedness of his father? (Genesis 9:22).
Cham the father of Canaan saw the nakedness of his father, and told it to his brothers [who were] Outside [in the marketplace].

“Ḥam, father of Canaan, saw the nakedness of his father, and told his two brothers outside” (Genesis 9:22). “Ḥam, father of Canaan, saw [and told [vayaged]]” – he said it to them and spoke persuasively to them;28Vayaged is interpreted in its Aramaic sense, of causing something to move forward, in this case, Ham’s brothers’ opinions. he said to them: Adam the first man had [only] two sons and one…

Ham, father of Canaan, saw the nakedness of his father. Canaan is mentioned here due to his involvement in the affair, as maintained by the Sages. Either Ham was the one who glanced inside the tent and went off and told his brothers, or he performed even worse actions. And all the days of Jared were nine hundred sixty and two years: and he died.
